source: Learning Ansible with Rocky - Ansible Basics: The ansible command line and modules

questions.q: What is the general form of the ansible ad-hoc command?
questions.why: The ad-hoc command runs a module against a host pattern with optional args and options.

questions.q: List the hosts belonging to the rocky8 group.
questions.why: --list-hosts shows which hosts a pattern matches, without doing anything to them.

questions.q: Ping every host in the rocky8 group using the ping module.
questions.why: -m ping runs the ping module and expects a pong back.

questions.q: Display the gathered facts of the rocky8 group.
questions.why: The setup module returns the host's facts.

questions.q: Which option runs an ad-hoc command with root privileges and prompts for the password?
questions.why: -b becomes root (privilege escalation) and -K asks for the escalation password.

options: Simulation (check mode): reports what would change without changing anything
Copies a file to the target
questions.why: -C is check/simulation mode: it tests the target without making changes.

questions.q: Rather than reaching for the shell module, the docs advise you to...
options: Look for a dedicated module that fits the need
Always use the command module instead
Write and copy a bash script
questions.why: There is a module for almost every need; prefer it over the generic shell module.

questions.q: Which module installs packages on a Rocky/RHEL target?
questions.why: dnf (or yum) manages RPM packages; apt is the Debian family, copy handles files, systemd manages services.

questions.q: After installing httpd with the dnf module, which module starts the service?
questions.why: The systemd module manages service state; name=httpd state=started starts it.
